original_abstract: This review examines the pivotal role of *in situ* UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy in deciphering the complexities of catalytic systems. We discuss an array of experimental methodologies designed to identify reaction mechanisms, determine kinetics, and probe structural properties across enzymatic, homogeneous, heterogeneous, and photocatalysis. Techniques such as stopped-flow, laser-driven transient absorption, and experimental perturbations are evaluated for their ability to access diverse timescales and accommodate various reactor configurations. In the realm of photocatalysis, we highlight the use of femto- and nanosecond-resolved measurements to track the dynamics of excited states. For structural elucidation, the review emphasizes the necessity of multi-spectroscopic approaches—exemplified by the synergy between magnetic circular dichroism and resonance Raman spectroscopy—to characterize active sites in metalloenzymes and transition-metal-exchanged zeolites. We further explore the tracking of redox states, oxygen vacancies, and band gaps in heterogeneous catalysts, with a khusus focus on electrocatalysis. Despite the inherent challenge of broad, convoluted absorption bands, we demonstrate how advanced data analysis can successfully monitor complex processes, such as coking on acid zeolites. Ultimately, while UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy is already fundamental to catalyst characterization and mechanistic investigation, its potential for broader application remains significant.
humanized_abstract: This review focuses on the power of in situ UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectroscopy in tackling the complexity of catalytic systems. This review discusses various experimental methods which have been employed for the elucidation of catalytic reaction mechanisms and kinetic studies, and the structure characterization of enzymes, as well as heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts in catalytic reactions such as photocatalysis. Experimental methods ranging from stopped-flow to laser-driven transient absorption as well as other methods involving perturbation have been employed for investigations over wide ranges of timescales applicable to almost all types of catalytic systems. In the case of photocatalysis, femto- and nanosecond-resolved experiments of the excited state species are often necessary for understanding the mechanisms and thus shorter timescale information are derived experimentally. The characterization of structure in catalysts is often also challenging as multi-spectroscopic approach are required. A combination of techniques such as magnetic circular dichroism and resonance Raman has enabled detailed characterization of metal centers in certain metalloenzymes, while characterization of the active sites of transition-metal-exchanged zeolites such as zeolite ZSM-5 required *in situ* MCD and Resonance Raman studies. Information such as the redox states of electrocatalysts, the oxygen vacancies on the surface of heterogeneous catalysts such as MOFs, sulfides, and perovskites and the band gaps of different nanomaterials especially those associated with the heterogeneous catalysts including those with electrocatalytic function is as well provided and highlighted as such in this review. Finally, in spite of the difficulties of broadband peaks associated with various species often attached to the surfaces of heterogeneous catalysts, which often leads to not tracking the occurrence of catalytic reaction on the surface, various strategies that have been employed in understanding various surface catalytic phenomena through various experiments involving *in situ* UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectroscopy have shown great promises of which needs further exploration in catalysts science.
